Topic 5: Part 1 (differing objectives & the Strait)

[167:31]And it's been a pretty dynamic situation.

[167:52]We are considering their peace proposal.

[168:08]And so when we reviewed the timeline last Thursday, we talked about how the United States

[168:13]submits a proposal.

[168:15]And what we say is, we're going to end our blockade over the straight, Iran will open

[168:21]We might defer the nuclear issue to a later day and we're going to bring an end to all

[168:26]the fighting in the Middle East.

[168:29]Iran comes back with their response and Iran says, not good enough.

[168:33]Iran says we want reparations.

[168:36]We want an end to the fighting in Lebanon.

[168:48]And I would summarize it as follows.

[168:52]The United States, it is imperative for us in this negotiation to achieve two things.

[169:03]And note what I mean by that.

[169:06]You're hearing this phrase thrown around a lot

[169:08]and we need to open the straight.

[169:12]That's not actually specific enough.

[169:29]the perilous journey.

[169:31]It's a very narrow choke point.

[169:40]So they have shut down all the commercial shipping going through, the Strait is effectively closed,

[169:46]and on the United States side, we have threatened to attack any authorized Iranian shipping that goes

Topic 6: Part 2 (another truce & a slow retreat)

[175:35]Iran is not going to give up nuclear.

[175:37]They're not going to give up the straight.

[175:39]And the United States can't leave without securing either of those things.

[175:43]So this weekend, it looked like the United States and Israel were preparing to go back

[175:47]to war.

[175:49]Trump has been threatening this for the past two months.

[175:51]He has repeatedly insisted that if Iran does not make a deal on some arbitrary timeline

[175:57]that eventually we would hit them again.

[176:01]And what that looks like is subject to a lot of speculation about what we are willing to

[176:09]Trump has talked about invading the mainland, invading the islands, or escalating the bombing

[176:15]against Iran's civilian infrastructure, their oil industry, their carg island, where they

[176:21]export all the oil, or just resuming the bombing of military and regime targets, potentially

[176:27]more assassination strikes.

[176:29]And so Trump has been bluffing for months that if Iran doesn't come to the table, we're

[176:33]going back in, and it seemed like maybe this weekend that was actually going to be the

[176:38]Well, Saturday, it was announced that the US and Iran were on the precipice of reaching

[176:44]a deal.

[176:45]They're calling it a memorandum of understanding, an MOU.

[176:49]And so this is not a formal deal.

[176:52]It's not going to be ratified by Congress or anything like that.

[176:56]But this is something like a truce.

[176:59]It's a ceasefire.

[177:00]It's a very informal way of ending the conflict.

[177:08]Saturday night, there were all kinds of rumors going around and they effectively said that

[177:13]Iran had agreed to give up their highly enriched uranium.

[177:19]And they said among other things that, that the United States would allow Iran to keep

[177:23]control over the straight and that Iran would be allowed to charge a toll over the straight.

[177:54]there was actually no agreement.

[180:58]But we are in this phase of the conflict where I don't even know how you would characterize

[181:03]it other than it seems that the United States is absolutely losing.

[181:08]There's no question about that.

[181:10]The United States is absolutely losing this war.

[181:14]We are in a mode right now where we are pursuing a unilateral surrender.

[181:19]We are begging Iran to make peace.

[181:21]We are begging Iran to open up the straight.

[181:24]We are making threats that we know are ineffectual, that we know we probably can't even follow

[181:30]through on.

[181:32]Even if we did, they wouldn't change Iran's behavior.

[181:38]So we truly seem to be in a position where we have no leverage.

[181:43]We have none of the cards.

[181:45]We have no military options.

[181:47]There is nothing we can do to compel Iran to negotiate.

[181:52]There is nothing we can do that prevents Iran from closing the straight.

[182:01]We are in a position where we are effectively at their mercy and we are just scrambling looking for something, some kind of creative solution, some kind of creative military option, where we can get out of this brutal reality, but we're just not finding it.

[182:17]They don't believe our bluff.

[182:20]because we have no credibility.

[182:22]They know we have no military options.

[182:25]This blockade of the Persian Gulf that we have going,

[182:28]it might be inflicting some economic pain,

[182:31]but they're in an existential war for survival,

[182:35]so they can absolutely tolerate that.

[182:37]Meanwhile, the global economy is still suffering

[182:41]from this energy shock, which is ongoing.

[182:44]So, this is an unequivocal strategic defeat for the United States, and it seems like now

[182:51]the retreat is just happening in slow motion.

[182:54]There is obviously this very strong desire for a withdrawal.

[183:00]The United States wants this to be over,

[183:02]but they cannot make it happen without losing face.

[183:06]And that is something maybe they haven't accepted yet.

[183:08]So it's just happening slowly over time.

[183:11]And the reason I say this is because every one

[183:14]of these developments follows the exact same pattern,

[183:17]which is we hear a statement from the president

[183:24]And they will say something like, Iran wants to make a deal more than anyone knows.

[183:31]We are negotiating with Iran.

[183:33]We are in late stages of a deal.

[183:35]A deal is imminent.

[183:36]They have agreed to all of these things in principle or in spirit.

[183:42]This is always how it starts.

[183:44]How many times have we heard that in the past 60 days?

[183:46]I think we've heard it no fewer than 10 times.

[183:49]We get one of these press gaggles where Trump says those words, they want to deal more than

[183:55]anyone knows.

[183:56]They're begging us for an extension so that a deal might possibly happen.

[184:02]They have made concessions on key issues.

[184:04]He doesn't use those words, but that is what is planted in the press.

[184:09]And then, inevitably, what follows is the Iranian response, which is they will come

[184:14]back and they will continue to say, as they have since the beginning, these are our terms.

[184:20]Take them or leave them.

[184:22]We're going to control the straight.

[184:23]We're not giving up our nuclear program.

[184:26]We're not giving up our missiles.

[184:28]We're not giving up our proxies.

[184:30]We want money and we want you out of here.

[184:33]And that has been their position, that has been their proposal to end the conflict since the conflict started and hasn't changed at all.

[184:41]Those have always been the key ingredients, even since the first ceasefire was promulgated.

[184:52]We're not negotiating over nuclear.

[184:54]And we want US forces to withdraw from the region, whether that's from the bases or

[184:59]at the minimum to pull back the blue water Navy that's stationed in the Arabian Sea.

[185:12]is lying about what they are saying.

[185:15]Trump administration says Iran begged for an extension, Iran says never happened.

[185:21]Trump administration says they agreed to give up their nuclear centrifuges.

[185:26]Iran says never happened.

[185:28]And as a matter of fact, we'll never give it up.

[185:31]Trump administration says we are in advanced negotiations, late negotiations, a deal is

[185:37]Iran says, no, a deal is not imminent.

[185:47]We don't want to talk anymore.

[185:50]This has been the tempo.

[185:51]This has been the pattern since the very beginning.

[185:59]And I will reiterate, we've talked about this a little bit, Iran is never going to give

Topic 7: Part 3 (Iran's deterrence & the uranium deal)

[186:04]up control over the straight, because that is part of their deterrence against a future

[186:11]US attack.

[186:12]The United States has not achieved any of its strategic objectives in this war.

[186:17]They have not achieved regime change.

[186:19]They have not destroyed Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium.

[186:23]Have not destroyed Iran's nuclear complex.

[186:27]Have not significantly even degraded Iran's missile capability.

[186:32]They have achieved none of these things.

[186:42]get right back to work and they already have rebuilding all these capabilities.

[186:51]They are gonna dig up the highly enriched uranium.

[186:54]They probably have centrifuges we don't know about and they will continue enrichment.

[186:58]They will build more drones and missiles.

[187:57]The US and Israel are going to keep coming back.

[188:00]We hit them last year, and then we came back this year.

[188:04]If we end the conflict now, we're probably going to come back a year from now, or two

[188:18]And as long as the United States is under Israel's influence, then it's only a matter

[188:26]There's another assassination, air raid, et cetera.

[188:37]it would be politically impossible for the United States to return.

[188:41]How do they do that?

[188:44]So that Washington, and by the way, everybody in the world knows, if the US and Israel get

[188:50]back up to their old tricks, it's going to be a repeat of this energy shock.

[188:55]If the US and Israel come back in Iran, try to take another crack at it.

[189:01]It's going to be a meltdown in Asia.

[189:02]It's going to be a meltdown in Europe.

[189:04]It's going to be a disaster for the golf and it's ultimately going to be a disaster for

[189:08]American consumers.

[189:13]They needed to deter a future attack.

[189:16]And the same goes for their nuclear program.

[189:18]Ultimately, the reason they have fought and sacrificed so much to maintain this nuclear

[189:23]threshold status is because that is their ultimate hedge against a US or Israel attack.

[189:32]What prevents even a nuclear strike by Israel or the United States against Iran?

[189:40]If we cannot invade, if we cannot destroy their stockpile that they have, Iran can make a nuke, they have parity with us, and then they can annihilate Israel or the United States.

[189:52]So they're never giving up that either because that is also part of their deterrent capability.

[189:57]So we are asking them to do something.

[190:01]Give up their nukes, give up the straight, that basically guarantees they will be vulnerable

[190:07]to a future attack, which most likely will be another attempted regime change.

[190:22]and getting the straight back because we ultimately seek regime change in Iran.

[190:27]We ultimately are pursuing a policy of nonproliferation and freedom of navigation in the seas.

[191:56]They're gonna make money off of it because they have jurisdiction over it.

[193:23]Here is the wild card in this entire arrangement, and I said this even last year.

Topic 8: Part 4 (Israel's role, sabotage & the Lebanon strike)

[193:29]So much of the discussion about the United States and Iran,

[193:34]I feel like up until this point ignores the wild card,

[193:48]every event that has taken place,

[193:50]and that is the interest of Israel.

[200:02]And let's just say something, let's just say something very explicit, which is that

[200:10]if Israel did not exist, the US and Iran would have already had a deal by now.

[200:16]They would have had a deal in 2003.

[200:20]They would have had a deal in 2009.

[200:22]They would have had a deal in 2015.

[200:24]They would have had a deal last year.

[200:27]If Israel did not exist, the United States and Iran would not be at war and wouldn't

[200:33]have been at war for years, maybe for over a decade.

[200:43]And the reason is because, as we've said before, it is Israel's interest that diplomacy does not succeed.

[200:52]Israel does not want peace. They do not want a deal. They do not want Iran to be a non-nuclear, prosperous state.

[201:03]Why is that? Because Iran is a very powerful country because they're a very big country with a very large population and they are a very rich country.

[201:14]They have 90 million people, lots of territory, lots of oil. They have a huge middle class. They have great universities, great scientists.

[201:25]and they have a sense of identity. The country is 90% Persian. It's not that diverse. And they have a

[201:32]sense of nationality, a sense of identity, which allows them to stick together. Their society is

[201:39]insular, not trusting of outsiders, meaning it's difficult to infiltrate and subvert.

[201:47]So it's not that Israel fears a nuclear Iran or an Iran armed with missiles.

[201:53]or in Iran armed with drones or controlling the strait or anything like that.

[201:58]What Israel will not tolerate is a powerful country in their neighborhood

[202:05]that exists as a rival.

[202:08]They will not tolerate the existence of a rival.

[202:12]Why? Because there are millions of Shiite Muslims across the Middle East, in Syria, in

[202:20]Iraq, in Bahrain, in the southeastern province, in the northwest coast of Yemen.

[202:26]If Iran is a stable, prosperous country, not only are they a hub of power and culture and

[202:42]They can influence the affairs in other states in a word. They are a regional player. They're a regional power

[202:50]This is what Israel will not tolerate

[202:53]And it was promulgated in the 1980s

[202:57]Israel's grand strategy is that they will not accept the existence of powerful Muslim or Arab states in their periphery

[203:07]This is why they destroyed Syria. This is why they destroyed Iraq. This is why they destroyed Libya

[203:14]This is why they pacified Egypt because all of these countries were strong Arab

[203:42]Their preference is to dismember those countries, turn them into statelets.

[203:47]They want to break them apart.

[203:49]They want to degrade and weaken them so that they will be under the control of Israel rather

[203:58]So they have had Iran in their sights for decades and they want to destroy Iran. This is why Iran has nukes

[204:06]This is why Iran has missiles. This is why Iran has its network of proxies because they are in a

[204:14]power struggle for regional dominance with Israel

[205:21]and it would not have even been a problem.

Topic 9: Part 5 (restraining Israel & it's them)

[206:13]So, ask yourself, where are we going?

[206:17]We were not in a war with Iran a year and a half ago.

[206:21]And five years before that, we were in a deal with Iran.

[206:24]And five years before that, we had actually a working relationship with Iran on this deal.

[206:32]But what got us to this point?

[206:34]It was this concerted effort over the long term by Israel to poison this relationship

[206:41]so that after a period of 10, 15 years, our only option increasingly is war.

[206:48]In 2015, 2013, we had a lot of options about how we wanted to go about this.

[206:54]But every step down this path has narrowed

[206:58]the field of possibilities.

[207:01]And so after Trump ripped up the deal,

[207:03]we had fewer options.

[207:05]And after he killed Qasem Soleimani,

[207:07]we had even fewer options.

[207:08]And after Israel engaged in their industrial sabotage

[207:11]and assassinations in the early 2020s,
